ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails db:migrate'

file.content:
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
# check=error=true

# This Dockerfile is designed for production, not development. Use with Kamal or build'n'run by hand:
# docker build -t sandcastle .
# docker run -d -p 80:80 -e RAILS_MASTER_KEY=<value from config/master.key> --name sandcastle sandcastle

# For a containerized dev environment, see Dev Containers: https://guides.rubyonrails.org/getting_started_with_devcontainer.html

# Make sure RUBY_VERSION matches the Ruby version in .ruby-version
ARG RUBY_VERSION=4.0.0
FROM docker.io/library/ruby:$RUBY_VERSION-slim AS base

# Rails app lives here
WORKDIR /rails

# Install base packages
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y curl libjemalloc2 libvips libpq5 postgresql-client openssh-client && \
    ln -s /usr/lib/$(uname -m)-linux-gnu/libjemalloc.so.2 /usr/local/lib/libjemalloc.so && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Set production environment variables and enable jemalloc for reduced memory usage and latency.
ENV RAILS_ENV="production" \
    BUNDLE_DEPLOYMENT="1" \
    BUNDLE_PATH="/usr/local/bundle" \
    BUNDLE_WITHOUT="development" \
    LD_PRELOAD="/usr/local/lib/libjemalloc.so"

# Throw-away build stage to reduce size of final image
FROM base AS build

# Install packages needed to build gems
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y build-essential git libpq-dev libyaml-dev pkg-config && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Install application gems
COPY Gemfile Gemfile.lock vendor ./

RUN bundle install && \
    rm -rf ~/.bundle/ "${BUNDLE_PATH}"/ruby/*/cache "${BUNDLE_PATH}"/ruby/*/bundler/gems/*/.git && \
    # -j 1 disable parallel compilation to avoid a QEMU bug: https://github.com/rails/bootsnap/issues/495
    bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 --gemfile

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Precompile bootsnap code for faster boot times.
# -j 1 disable parallel compilation to avoid a QEMU bug: https://github.com/rails/bootsnap/issues/495
RUN bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 app/ lib/

# Precompiling assets for production without requiring secret RAILS_MASTER_KEY
RUN SECRET_KEY_BASE_DUMMY=1 ./bin/rails assets:precompile


# Development stage for live source mounting
FROM base AS development

# Install development dependencies
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
    build-essential \
    git \
    libpq-dev \
    node-gyp \
    pkg-config \
    python-is-python3 \
    ca-certificates \
    gnupg && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Install Docker CLI
RUN install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings && \
    curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/debian/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/debian $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list && \
    apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y docker-ce-cli && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Create user early
RUN groupadd --system --gid 220568 sandcastle && \
    useradd sandcastle --uid 220568 --gid 220568 --create-home --shell /bin/bash && \
    groupadd --system docker && \
    usermod -aG docker sandcastle

# Switch to development bundle config
ENV RAILS_ENV="development" \
    BUNDLE_PATH="/usr/local/bundle" \
    BUNDLE_WITHOUT=""

# Install all gems (including development/test)
COPY Gemfile Gemfile.lock ./
# Install libyaml-dev for psych gem
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y libyaml-dev && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives
RUN bundle install

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /rails

# Run as sandcastle user
USER 220568:220568

# Entrypoint for db readiness
ENTRYPOINT ["/rails/bin/docker-entrypoint"]

# Default to bin/dev (Foreman with Rails + Tailwind)
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["./bin/dev"]


# Final stage for app image
FROM base

# Install Docker CLI for container management via mounted socket
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y ca-certificates gnupg && \
    install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings && \
    curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/debian/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/debian $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list && \
    apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y docker-ce-cli sudo btrfs-progs && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Run and own only the runtime files as a non-root user for security
RUN groupadd --system --gid 220568 sandcastle && \
    useradd sandcastle --uid 220568 --gid 220568 --create-home --shell /bin/bash && \
    groupadd --system docker && \
    usermod -aG docker sandcastle && \
    printf 'sandcastle ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/btrfs subvolume *\nsandcastle ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/chown\n' > /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle && \
    chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/sandcastle
USER 220568:220568

# Copy built artifacts: gems, application
COPY --chown=sandcastle:sandcastle --from=build "${BUNDLE_PATH}" "${BUNDLE_PATH}"
COPY --chown=sandcastle:sandcastle --from=build /rails /rails

# Build metadata for version footer
ARG BUILD_VERSION
ARG BUILD_GIT_SHA
ARG BUILD_GIT_DIRTY
ARG BUILD_DATE
ENV BUILD_VERSION=${BUILD_VERSION} \
    BUILD_GIT_SHA=${BUILD_GIT_SHA} \
    BUILD_GIT_DIRTY=${BUILD_GIT_DIRTY} \
    BUILD_DATE=${BUILD_DATE}

# Entrypoint prepares the database.
ENTRYPOINT ["/rails/bin/docker-entrypoint"]

# OCI labels for ghcr.io package linking
LABEL service="sandcastle"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/thieso2/sandcastle"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.description="Self-hosted shared Docker sandbox platform"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.licenses="MIT"

# Start server via Thruster by default, this can be overwritten at runtime
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["./bin/thrust", "./bin/rails", "server"]

file.content:
#!/bin/bash -e

# Wait for PostgreSQL to be ready (belt-and-suspenders with compose health check)
if [ -n "$DB_HOST" ]; then
  echo "Waiting for PostgreSQL at $DB_HOST..."
  for i in $(seq 1 30); do
    if pg_isready -h "$DB_HOST" -U "${DB_USER:-sandcastle}" -q 2>/dev/null; then
      break
    fi
    sleep 1
  done
fi

# If running the rails server then create or migrate existing database
if [ "${@: -2:1}" == "./bin/rails" ] && [ "${@: -1:1}" == "server" ]; then
  ./bin/rails db:prepare
fi

exec "${@}"

content.thinking:
The entrypoint runs `./bin/rails db:prepare` on startup — so migrations should run automatically. But wait, the CMD in the Dockerfile is `./bin/thrust ./bin/rails server`, not `./bin/rails server` directly.

The check in the entrypoint is:
```bash
if [ "${@: -2:1}" == "./bin/rails" ] && [ "${@: -1:1}" == "server" ]; then
```

This checks if the second-to-last argument is `./bin/rails` and the last argument is `server`. But the CMD is `["./bin/thrust", "./bin/rails", "server"]`.


With that array, the second-to-last argument is indeed `./bin/rails` and the last is `server`, so the condition should match and trigger the database preparation.

But if `db:prepare` runs migrations only when the schema exists, and the database already has other tables, then the migration should have executed. The real issue might be that the container wasn't restarted with the new image after deployment, or the migration file simply wasn't present when the container last started. run `db:migrate` on existing databases. So if the migration file exists in the image and the container is restarted, it should pick it up.

But the issue could be: the image was deployed (container restarted with new code including the controller), but `db:prepare` might have failed silently, or the migration file was added after the container was last started.

Actually, the real question is why this hasn't run on container startup — and looking at the code, it SHOULD have run via `db:prepare` in the entrypoint. If it didn't, the most likely culprits are: the container wasn't restarted after the new image was deployed, `db:prepare` failed silently, or the migration was added to the codebase but the image wasn't rebuilt and redeployed.

Now looking at the actual situation — the error is happening in production right now, which means the code with `Admin::InvitesController` is running but the `invites` table doesn't exist. This points to either the migration failing silently during `db:prepare`, the container being restarted without the image containing the migration file, or the release process pushing a new image that somehow doesn't include the migration.

Looking at the Dockerfile more carefully, I'm checking the entrypoint logic that determines when to run `db:prepare`. The entrypoint should have run `db:prepare` on startup, which includes migrations, so that should've worked. But if the old image were still running, we'd see a routing error for the missing controller instead of a database error — so the new code is definitely deployed, which means the migration ran but something's off, or the migration file itself has an issue. the entrypoint again, which means `db:prepare` would run — unless the files were patched directly into the container without restarting, which would skip the entrypoint entirely. restarting (just `docker cp` without restart), to hot-patch the running container. In that case, the container would have the new Ruby files but the entrypoint wouldn't have run again with `db:prepare`.

OK I think the most likely explanation is one of:
1. Files were hot-patched into the running container without a restart (so entrypoint/db:prepare never ran)
2. `db:prepare` ran but errored silently

But actually, re-reading: the entrypoint uses `set -e` (`#!/bin/bash -e`), so if `db:prepare` fails, the container would exit. The fact that the container is running means either `db:prepare` succeeded (but skipped the migration somehow?) or it wasn't run at all.

I think the most direct answer is: looking at the entrypoint, `db:prepare` SHOULD run migrations on startup. If it didn't, either the container was patched without a restart, or there's a deploy process issue. The best move is to just run the migration manually now.

content.text:
It should have — the entrypoint runs `db:prepare` on startup (line 16). But the condition checks specifically for `./bin/rails server` as the last two args, which matches the CMD `["./bin/thrust", "./bin/rails", "server"]`. So the logic is correct.

The likely culprits:

1. **Hot-patched without restart** — if someone used the `scp`/`docker cp` method to push files without a `docker restart`, the entrypoint never re-ran and `db:prepare` was skipped.

2. **`db:prepare` errored and the container exited**, then something (like `restart: unless-stopped` in compose) restarted it — but on a restart Docker re-runs the same command *without* the entrypoint on some setups. Actually no, `ENTRYPOINT` always runs — but if the DB wasn't ready yet on the first fast restart cycle, `pg_isready` could timeout and `db:prepare` could fail, causing the container to exit and loop.

3. **The new image wasn't actually deployed yet** — the controller code reached production via hot-patch, but a full image rebuild/redeploy hasn't happened, so the migration file was never in the image when `db:prepare` last ran.

The safest fix right now is to run it manually:

```bash
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails db:migrate'
```

Then verify with:

```bash
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails db:migrate:status'
```

- `Create invites` — shows `up` (because `20260219000001` is in `schema_migrations`) but the table **doesn't actually exist**
- `Create snapshots` — shows `down`, same ID

What happened: Rails recorded `20260219000001` in `schema_migrations` (likely from a previous run or the snapshots migration), so it thinks `Create invites` already ran — but it never did.

file.content:
class CreateSnapshots < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  def change
    create_table :snapshots do |t|
      t.belongs_to :user, null: false, foreign_key: true
      t.string :name, null: false
      t.string :label
      t.string :source_sandbox
      t.string :docker_image
      t.string :home_snapshot
      t.string :data_snapshot
      t.string :data_subdir
      t.bigint :docker_size
      t.bigint :home_size
      t.bigint :data_size

      t.timestamps
    end

    add_index :snapshots, [ :user_id, :name ], unique: true
  end
end

input.description: Rename create_snapshots migration to avoid timestamp collision

content.text:
Now in production, we also need to fix the `schema_migrations` table — the `20260219000001` entry was recorded for snapshots (which never created the invites table), so we need to delete that record and re-run both migrations:

```bash
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails db:migrate'
```

But that alone won't work since Rails still thinks `20260219000001` (invites) already ran. We need to remove the stale entry first:

```bash
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails runner "ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute(\"DELETE FROM schema_migrations WHERE version = '20260219000001'\")"'
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker exec sandcastle-web bin/rails db:migrate'
```

This will:
1. Remove the stale `20260219000001` from `schema_migrations`
2. Re-run `create_invites` (20260219000001) and `create_snapshots` (now 20260219000002)

The local fix (renaming the file) should be committed so the collision doesn't recur.

message.content: commit and apply the fix in production

The quickest fix is to patch the running container: remove the old duplicate file and copy the new one in.

Let me use the "Patching Live" approach from CLAUDE.md:
```bash
scp path/to/file.rb sandcastle@sandman:/tmp/file.rb
ssh sandcastle@sandman 'docker cp /tmp/file.rb sandcastle-web:/rails/path/to/file.rb && docker restart sandcastle-web'


```

Actually, I can simplify this — I just need to remove the old migration file and run db:migrate again since the schema_migrations entry was already cleaned up. No restart needed.

'

Good progress! The `invites` table was successfully created! The `create_invites` migration ran successfully. Now the problem is that the `snapshots` table already exists (it was created by the old `20260219000001_create_snapshots.rb` that ran previously). So we need to mark the `create_snapshots` migration as already run without actually running it.

I need to mark `20260219000002` as already run in schema_migrations.

content.text: Good news — `create_invites` ran successfully. The snapshots table already exists in the DB (it ran before under the old ID). Just need to mark `20260219000002` as done without running it:
